audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Numbers / Bemidbar Peor crisis, Phinehas killing, plague, and covenant-of-peace text tradition, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Shittim, Moabite worship invitations, Baal Peor allegiance, execution command, Zimri and Cozbi, Phinehas' spear killing, plague total, covenant of peace/priesthood, and anti-Midian command. Maximum anti-violence, anti-misogyny, anti-xenophobia, and anti-religious-persecution safeguards required.

verses.restored: Israel stayed in Shittim, and people began to prostitute themselves/act unfaithfully with daughters of Moab.
They called people to sacrifices of their gods, and people ate and bowed down to their gods.
Israel yoked itself to Baal of Peor, and anger of YHWH burned against Israel.
YHWH said to Moses, Take all heads/leaders of people and expose/impale them before YHWH in sun, so burning anger of YHWH may turn back from Israel.
Moses said to judges of Israel, Each of you kill his men who yoked themselves to Baal of Peor.

Look, a man from children of Israel came and brought a Midianite woman near to his brothers, before eyes of Moses and before eyes of all congregation of children of Israel, while they were weeping at entrance of tent of meeting.
Phinehas son of Eleazar, son of Aaron the priest, saw, rose from midst of congregation, and took spear in his hand.
He went after Israelite man into inner chamber/tent and pierced both of them, Israelite man and woman through her body/belly; and plague was restrained from children of Israel.
Those who died in plague were twenty-four thousand.
Phinehas son of Eleazar, son of Aaron the priest, turned back my wrath from children of Israel by being zealous with my zeal in their midst, so I did not consume children of Israel in my zeal.
Therefore say: Look, I give him my covenant of peace/shalom.
It shall be for him and for his seed after him a covenant of age-long priesthood, because he was zealous for his Divine and made atonement/covering for children of Israel.
Name of Israelite man who was struck, who was struck with Midianite woman, was Zimri son of Salu, leader of a fathers' house among Simeonites.
Name of Midianite woman who was struck was Cozbi daughter of Zur; he was head of clans, a fathers' house in Midian.

for they harassed you with their schemes by which they schemed against you concerning Peor and concerning Cozbi their sister, daughter of a leader of Midian, who was struck on day of plague concerning Peor.

translation_notes: The chapter begins with Israelite participation in Moabite worship; the men are moral agents, and women must not be made sole scapegoats for the crisis.
Moabite women in verses 1-3 and the named Midianite woman Cozbi in verses 6-18 should not be carelessly collapsed into one ethnic-sexual stereotype.
The wording of verse 4 is difficult: 'heads/leaders' and the exposure/impalement command require lexical and legal-history audit.
The text names both Zimri and Cozbi and identifies both as members of leading families; Cozbi is not an anonymous seductress.
The exact nature of Zimri and Cozbi's act in the chamber is not fully stated. Later sexual readings should not be treated as the only lexical possibility.

Phinehas' extrajudicial double killing followed by a covenant of peace is among Torah's most morally dangerous reception texts. It must never be reenacted, imitated, praised as modern vigilantism, or used against interfaith, interracial, queer, or sexual relationships.
Plague language is ancient judgment narrative and must not be mapped onto sexually transmitted infections, epidemics, infertility, or modern moral blame.
The command against Midian is collective-war language that continues in Numbers 31 and requires categorical anti-genocide and anti-xenophobia safeguards.
safety_note: Never use Numbers 25 to justify zealot violence, honor killing, murder of couples, religious persecution, misogyny, anti-Midian or anti-Arab hatred, opposition to interfaith or interracial relationships, sexual shaming, or collective punishment. Preserve the source honestly while treating 'covenant of peace' as a text demanding the end—not repetition—of bloodshed.
